To combat wiki spam, I've implemented a posting throttle on my blog to mitigate the issue. However, this approach is insufficient, as new spam-capable zombie PCs and outdated wikis can quickly render it ineffective. If I were to revisit my wiki after a month, it would likely be overrun with spam links. A more robust solution is the concept of distributed social whitelists, which I've dubbed FOAFKey. This technology enables a whitelist of authorized users to post to a wiki or weblog without restrictions, requiring only their email address or SHA1 hash for verification. In cases where users are not part of the original whitelist, a simple upload process can be employed.

I've developed a novel approach to combating wiki spam by leveraging distributed social whitelists, which I've dubbed FOAFKey. In the rare instances where users are not part of the original whitelist, a straightforward upload process can be utilized.
